2026-02-20.assessment: First Meta-tied news in five weeks: Evergy disclosed 1.9GW of new electric service contracts explicitly naming Meta and Google, with at least one more contract expected this year - a fresh, concrete milestone that partially resets the silence decay, though it's generation capacity broadly rather than nuclear-specific like the original Vistra/Oklo deals. This confirms the structural hyperscaler-power-buildout driver remains active and Meta is still actively contracting for capacity, not walking back commitments. No reversal of the driving condition; macro tape remains choppy on AI capex jitters but that's sentiment, not thesis-breaking. Vistra stays the cleanest direct nuclear-PPA beneficiary; Oklo's Meta-tied thread remains quieter but the broader theme just got a fresh data point supporting continued hyperscaler power demand.

2026-02-27.exit_case: none — MP just signed new automaker supply deal and government price-support continues, catalyst still unfolding
2026-02-27.assessment: MP Materials just posted a Q4 profit swing driven by US government price support and signed a major new automaker supply contract, plus announced a $1.25B Texas magnet facility — this is a structural buildout with fresh concrete milestones landing this week, not a one-off resolved event. The rare earths deal thesis is actively compounding with government backing and new commercial contracts still to be finalized (more automaker deals reportedly in pipeline). FCX and WPM are more peripheral (copper/silver royalty plays) but still loosely tied to critical-minerals supply chain re-shoring theme; PPTA remains a smaller pure-play peer.

2026-03-06.exit_case: none - AI demand still strengthening (Marvell, Broadcom, memory pricing), no reversal of driver
2026-03-06.assessment: Strong fresh confirmation this week: Marvell surges 18-20% on AI demand guidance ('do you see me blinking'), Broadcom posts strong results with $10B buyback, both reinforcing the structural AI-chip/memory demand driver. NVDA/AMD dip on new US export-permit rules for AI chips - a regulatory friction point but not a reversal of underlying demand (arguably reflects demand being so strong it needs export controls). Besi warning on chip-bonding slowdown is a minor negative datapoint but isolated. No datable resolution or reversal of AI memory/semiconductor demand; MRVL/AVGO earnings directly extend the AI-capex arc with fresh milestones. Basket unchanged; adding MRVL and AVGO as credible fresh pure-plays explicitly tied to this week's AI-demand catalyst confirmation.

2026-03-27.exit_case: Google TurboQuant compression threatens to cut future DRAM/storage demand growth, but unconfirmed & analysts say buy dip
2026-03-27.assessment: Fresh MU-specific news this week is negative in tone (Google's TurboQuant compression tech raising fears it could reduce memory/storage demand), causing a tumble in MU/SNDK, though sell-side analysts frame it as a buy-the-dip overreaction rather than a fundamental reversal. Broader tech selloff (Nasdaq's worst week in a year) is driven by Iran war escalation and Meta legal woes - macro/geopolitical noise unrelated to the AI-demand driver itself. No datable resolution or reversal of AI/memory demand; TurboQuant is a real risk to monitor going forward as a potential structural headwind, but it remains speculative/unconfirmed impact on actual chip orders, not a concrete demand reversal. This resets silence decay given direct fresh MU-specific coverage, but tone is now more mixed than prior weeks - holding conviction moderate given genuine uncertainty introduced. Basket unchanged; all six vehicles still credibly tied to the AI-chip/memory demand wave, though watch TurboQuant developments closely next week.

2026-05-08.exit_case: none - AI memory/chip demand driver still accelerating, not resolved or reversed
2026-05-08.assessment: Strong fresh confirmation this week: MU hits new all-time highs, MarketWatch/CNBC explicitly frame a broad 'return to AI data-center optimism' with AMD and Micron surging alongside Intel, and CNBC's 'changing of the guard in AI' piece names CPU/memory demand skyrocketing as AI moves from chatbots to agents - directly on-thesis and resetting silence decay. TipRanks MU vs SNDK piece confirms AI-driven memory demand continuing to soar in 2026. Power-grid strain coverage (PJM crisis due to AI) further reinforces the structural capacity buildout. Macro noise this week (Hormuz tensions, oil volatility, Iran peace-deal chatter) is unrelated geopolitical/commodity risk, not a semiconductor demand reversal. No datable resolution or reversal of the AI memory/chip demand driver; the arc continues to generate fresh milestones (new ATHs, sell-side target hikes, CPU demand rotation). Basket unchanged; all six vehicles still credibly ride the structural AI-chip/memory demand wave, with MU and AMD garnering the freshest direct confirmation this week.
2026-05-08.exit_advice: exit if AI capex/data-center demand growth visibly slows or memory pricing rolls over

2026-05-22.exit_case: none — structural driver intact; China's fresh Japan rare-earth cutoff and new US-Korea magnet JV reinforce, not reverse, thesis
2026-05-22.assessment: Fresh news this week breaks the silence: China's rare-earth cutoff to Japan escalates the geopolitical urgency underlying the structural Western re-shoring thesis, and a new $200M US-South Korea rare earth magnet JV (ReElement/POSCO, tied to AREC) is a concrete milestone in the broader alternative-supply-chain buildout. No counter-event (curbs lifted, deal unwound, price support ended) observed - driver remains structurally intact and reinforced. Conviction ticks up off the cull floor given genuine fresh coverage, though still modest given no MP-specific news. MP/PPTA remain cleanest pure-plays; AREC now directly relevant given the JV; FCX/WPM remain peripheral critical-minerals exposure.

2026-05-29.exit_case: Barchart piece argues MU's memory-deficit thesis has largely played out vs NVDA-style growth, but no concrete demand reversal confirmed
2026-05-29.assessment: Dell's blowout Q1'27 (+33%, AI server boom, hyperscaler spend acceleration) and SMCI's 16% pop provide fresh, strong confirmation of the structural AI-infrastructure demand driver this week, resetting silence decay - Cramer/CNBC frame it as setting up a 'crucial week for AI stocks.' MarketWatch notes Micron among top S&P soarers during the AI-driven May rally, directly on-thesis. SK Hynix crossing $1T market cap on AI boom further corroborates the memory-demand backdrop globally. One bearish note (Barchart: 'Micron isn't Nvidia, time to take foot off gas') argues the memory-deficit thesis has partly played out and urges caution, a valid fade signal but not a concrete reversal - memory pricing/demand growth is not shown rolling over; if anything Dell/SMCI results show accelerating downstream demand. Macro noise (Hormuz/Iran deal deliberations, oil, gold) remains unrelated geopolitical chatter, not a semiconductor demand data point. No datable resolution or reversal of the AI memory/chip demand driver; structural buildout continues generating fresh milestones (Dell/SMCI earnings, SK Hynix trillion-dollar valuation). Basket unchanged; all six vehicles still credibly ride the AI-chip/memory demand wave, with DELL's own earnings reinforcing the downstream server/demand angle even though DELL itself is not a core basket ticker here.

2026-06-19.assessment: Fresh, direct catalyst this week: Reuters/Yahoo/Global Banking & Finance all frame Micron's June 24 earnings as the key 'pulse check' for the entire AI rally, a concrete, datable near-term event that resets silence decay and reinforces MU as the central pure-play. Hormuz/Iran ceasefire developments (transit reopening, threat level reduced) are unrelated geopolitical de-escalation, not a semiconductor demand reversal - if anything easing macro tension is broadly risk-on for equities. Accenture's IT-spending cut hitting Indian IT stocks is a services-sector datapoint, not AI-chip/memory demand data, and doesn't touch MU/NVDA/AMD's hardware thesis. No datable resolution or reversal of the AI memory/chip demand driver; structural buildout continues with concrete near-term catalyst (MU earnings 6/24) still ahead. Basket unchanged - all six vehicles still credibly ride the AI-chip/memory demand wave.

2026-06-26.exit_case: none - Micron's blowout Q4 print confirms AI memory demand still accelerating, not reversing
2026-06-26.assessment: Micron's June 24-25 earnings beat-and-raise delivered exactly the concrete, datable catalyst flagged last week as the sector's 'pulse check,' triggering a broad tech rally (Yahoo: 'Micron Leads Tech Stocks Rally on Blowout Earnings') and directly validating the AI-memory demand thesis. However, the follow-through is choppy: Reuters notes chip stocks resuming their slide the next day on profit-taking, Bloomberg frames 'the AI trade still works but it's getting harder' amid extreme positioning, and Sky News/Business Insider flag rising investor anxiety about AI capex durability and a SoftBank-led Asia tech rout. This is volatility/rotation around a confirmed-strong fundamental print, not a reversal of the underlying demand driver - Micron itself just delivered hard evidence memory demand is accelerating. No datable resolution or reversal of the AI/memory demand catalyst; if anything this week supplies the strongest direct fundamental confirmation yet. Basket unchanged; all six vehicles still credibly ride the structural AI-chip/memory demand wave, with MU's own earnings now the freshest and most powerful milestone.
2026-06-26.exit_advice: exit if AI capex/data-center demand growth visibly slows or memory pricing rolls over

Bernstein raises MU target to $330 citing AI demand
Micron/Sandisk surge on unprecedented data center demand outlook

DRAM ETF piece frames memory as biggest AI bottleneck; Dell/SMCI blowout Q1 confirm downstream demand

2026-06-26.assessment: Twenty-fourth consecutive week with no direct US-Greenland dispute escalation coverage. Silence continues to compound the fade per silence-decay rules; conviction remains pinned at the cull floor after nearly six months of total silence on the specific US-Denmark/Greenland dispute. Underlying catalyst has not formally reversed (no deal, no explicit de-escalation statement), so thesis stays technically live but is an extremely weak candidate for continued holding.
